title: Koshari
subtitle: Egyptian street food of lentils, rice, pasta, and crispy onions
excerpt: Egypt's national dish: brown lentils and long-grain rice cooked together in one pot, topped with cooked pasta, a sharp tomato sauce spiked with vinegar and cumin, crispy fried onions, and a drizzle of garlic and chilli oil. Each component is made separately and layered in the bowl — the result is greater than any individual part.
sourceNotes: Koshari (also spelled koshary or kushari) is considered Egypt's national dish, sold by street vendors throughout Cairo and Alexandria from carts and small shops. Claudia Roden's 'A Book of Middle Eastern Food' (1968) documents it as a working-class Cairo street food. The dish combines elements from Italian (pasta), Indian (lentils), and Egyptian (rice, cumin) food traditions, reflecting Cairo's cosmopolitan nineteenth-century history.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: All components can be made ahead and reheated separately. The tomato sauce keeps for 3 days in the fridge. Fry the onions just before serving.

content.text: Koshari is an assembly dish. Each component is cooked independently and brought together in the bowl at serving time, which means each can be tasted and adjusted individually before the final layering. The tomato sauce needs to be sharp — more vinegar than you think, plenty of garlic and cumin. The crispy onions are the non-negotiable finish: slow-fried until deeply caramelised, then the heat raised to crisp them. Without the textural contrast of the onions, the dish is flat.

The lentils and rice are cooked together, absorbing the cooking liquid of the lentils. The pasta (small elbows or ditalini work best) is cooked separately and added on top. Some versions also include chickpeas — they add substance and are worth including.

Cook the {{lentils-brown}} in 700 ml salted water for 15 minutes until just tender. Add the rinsed {{long-grain-rice}} directly to the lentils and their cooking liquid. Add a little more water if needed to just cover. Bring to the boil, then cover and cook over the lowest possible heat for 15 minutes until the rice is cooked and the liquid is absorbed. Season with salt.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a large pan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 25 to 30 minutes, stirring occasionally, until deeply golden and jammy. Raise the heat to high and cook for a further 5 minutes, stirring, until the onions are crisp at the edges. Season with salt and drain on kitchen paper.

In a medium saucepan over medium heat, cook the {{garlic}} in 1 tablespoon of oil for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{coriander-ground}}, {{chilli-flakes}}, and {{red-wine-vinegar}}. Simmer for 15 minutes until slightly thickened. Season with salt. Taste — the sauce should be sharp and assertively seasoned.

Cook the {{pasta-dried}} according to the packet instructions until al dente. Drain. Warm the {{chickpeas-tinned}} if using. To serve: spoon lentil rice into bowls, add pasta and chickpeas on top, ladle over a generous amount of tomato sauce, and pile the crispy {{onion}} on top.

items.cause: Too much liquid or lentils overcooked before adding rice
items.fix: Cook the lentils only until just tender. The rice needs no more than about 300 ml liquid when added — boil down excess before adding the rice
Koshari sauce should be punchy. Add vinegar gradually and taste — it should be noticeably sharp. More cumin and a pinch of cayenne can sharpen the flavour

items.symptom: Onions are soft rather than crispy
items.cause: Not enough heat at the end
items.fix: The onions need the initial slow cook for sweetness and the final high-heat blast for crispness. Both stages are required
Koshari should be assembled to order just before eating, not left sitting. Cook the pasta at the last moment and serve immediately

content.text: Koshari is one of the great street foods of Cairo — cheap, filling, endlessly satisfying, sold by vendors from metal carts with the various components in separate containers, assembled to order and eaten standing up or in small koshari-only restaurants. Its origins are usually traced to the nineteenth century when Egypt, under colonial and cosmopolitan influence, absorbed elements from various food traditions into something distinctly Egyptian. The combination of lentils, rice, pasta, and tomato sauce may appear incongruous on paper but in the bowl it makes complete sense: each component is starchy and simple, but the sharp sauce and the crispy onion pull everything into focus. It is, as Claudia Roden noted, the food of the people.
